Um, so very interested to hear from you and it'd be good if you can leave some time for questions. Koto Koto 10 Coto call Laura Ansel. Hello everyone. My name is Laura Ansel. Um, I'd like to start my submission to you today by showing you three photos.

Um, this is my dad Billy, and he was a war baby. He was born in England in 1946 at the end of World War ii. And this is his biological father and my grandfather, uh, a New Zealander who was serving with the Royal New Zealand Air Force, uh, in England when he met my grandmother.

And my dad was conceived. Um, but my grandfather was repatriated back to New Zealand before my grandmother knew that she was pregnant. And my dad therefore was born out of wedlock and unfortunately, he never got to know his biological father. Um, suddenly my dad passed away when I was 17.

And on his deathbed, my mom and I made, made him a promise that we would find his biological family. So to cut a very long story short, using DNA extracted from biopsy samples that were stored in hospitals, um, from both my deceased grandfather and my deceased father, I was able to prove their father and son relationship and our lineage and connection with ERO New Zealand.

So it was quite an unusual thing to do. Um, but this is something that I'm very proud of my connection with this country and my longstanding family lineage here. And I'm extremely grateful for my new found family too. Um, so I was pretty shocked when the Department of Internal Affairs told me that because my dad was born outta wedlock, that he would not have had a claim to citizenship by dissent, uh, through his New Zealand father.

And then that has had a knock on effect for me today. So the department acknowledged that wedlock was an outdated concept and they noted that it wasn't part of our citizenship act today, the 19 77 1. Um, but what about those people born before then?

So this was what led me to start this petition because the consequences of those historical attitudes echo through generations preventing some individuals from rightfully claiming their New Zealand heritage today. Um, you will have seen the submission by the department.

Um, and on page five of that, um, it said that it was unaware of other cases like mine, and that is likely that my case was uncommon. However, in the DIA submission to the minister in reference to my application in 2023, it said, and I quote, the department is aware of older citizenship cases with multi-generational claims which had failed due to the issue of illegitimacy.

And it said, while Laura's circumstances are exceptional to her, they are not necessarily rare or outta the ordinary to the department. So you can see that these two statements by the DIA are contradictory. Now I totally acknowledge that the law at the time of my dad's birth was operating as the departments say, um, as it was intended.

In fact, I've read the parliamentary debates from 1948 in great detail, and the purpose of the wedlock requirement was because back then birth and marriage certificates were the only way to truly prove your connection and your lineage and where you came from. Sure. But today we have DNA, so it took me two citizenship applications, a complaint to the Human Rights Commission and a total of three years and eight months of trying to convince the DIA of my connection with New Zealand before I was finally granted citizenship.

So I have no skin in this game now because my fight, um, was eventually won, but it didn't need to be this difficult for my identity and my heritage to be formally acknowledged in a way that it would've been had my dad been born to married parents. Um, I don't feel that my dad was any less of a person because of those circumstances.

Um, but the reality is that he and I were treated differently to legitimate people, which is why I feel a duty to highlight and, um, change this inequity. Just going back to the submission from the DIA as well, I'd like to draw your attention to, um, their comment explaining that there is a pathway by nine one C for those who are born overseas and out of wedlock.

My submission to you is that this pathway is not actually fit for purpose when it comes to familial claims of lineage. The department's very own policy says that nine one C is a very difficult requirement to meet, and exceptional circumstances should be relatively rare and uncommon.

And my lived experience proves that because I initially applied under nine one C and was declined after the DIA concluded that my circumstances were not consistent with the department's interpretation. So again, there seems to be two different lines coming from the department.

Um, and they said that there was no evidence to support a benefit or advantage to New Zealand by making me a citizen. So I made the complaint to the Human Rights Commission that I was being discriminated against based on the marital status of my grandparents. Um, and the DIA suggested that I reapplied under nine one C had it not been for the fact that my dad was born out of wedlock, I would've met all the criteria for nine one B, which is the route that acknowledges familial connection, f**k papa and represents identity.

But I was excluded from applying in this way simply because my dad was considered illegitimate and that really hurt. Um, so this is, this issue isn't about rewriting history, nor is it about granting automatic citizenship to all who claim distant ancestry, but it is about acknowledging an unfair discrepancy and providing a proper pathway for those affected to apply for citizenship.

It's a matter of equity and ensuring people who would otherwise be eligible for New Zealand citizenship, that they're not arbitrarily excluded due to circumstances that were entirely out of their control, whether their parents were married or not at the time of their birth. And I think these are, you know, long considered irrelevant issues within our modern society in New Zealand.
